trials.justification: All 6 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-6, plus BR-7 as reinforcement of BR-1). Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1/BR-7, record visibility BR-5, note locking BR-6) correctly marked invariant: true. Cancellation rules BR-3/BR-4 correctly split by role with BR-3 (time window) as invariant: false and BR-4 (role boundary) as invariant: false — both defensible classifications. 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well exceeding the 4 minimum. Confidence level 72 is within the 65-90 range with clearly stated assumptions about license validation mechanism, regulatory framework, and record access scope.

observations.text: All 6 required business rules identified with correct invariant classifications. Regulatory rules consistently marked as invariants with strong justifications.

12 acceptance criteria — triple the minimum — covering all required scenarios: licensed physician booking (AC-1/AC-2), double-booking (AC-3), late cancellation (AC-4/AC-5/AC-6), and unauthorized record access (AC-7/AC-8).
Role-based cancellation correctly captured as two separate rules (BR-3 for patient time window, BR-4 for admin override), with the distinction clearly articulated.
Confidence level 72 with well-reasoned assumptions — correctly notes ambiguity around 'treating physician' scope, license revocation post-booking, and boundary conditions.
Exceptional edge case analysis covering concurrency (simultaneous booking), boundary conditions (exactly 2h/48h), back-to-back appointments, and license revocation — goes well beyond requirements.
Escalations are well-structured with concrete options and recommendations, particularly the medical record access scope ambiguity.

BR-3 and BR-4 both classified as invariant: false. While the 2-hour window (BR-3) is correctly soft, the role boundary in BR-4 could arguably be invariant: true since allowing patients to cancel inside the window undermines the scheduling protection. However, the justification is defensible — the specific role assignment is a policy choice.

trials.justification: All 6 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-8 cover all 6 expected rules, with cancellation split into patient/admin and note locking split into threshold/immutability). Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1/BR-5, record visibility BR-6, record lock immutability BR-8) correctly marked invariant: true. The 48-hour threshold (BR-7) marked invariant: false is a reasonable nuanced distinction separating the policy threshold from the immutability mechanism. Role-based cancellation correctly captured with BR-3 (patient, time-limited) and BR-4 (admin, unrestricted). 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum 4, covering all required scenarios: licensed physician booking (AC-1/AC-2), double-booking (AC-3), late cancellation (AC-5/AC-6), and unauthorized record access (AC-7/AC-8). Confidence at 72 is within the 65-90 range with clearly stated assumptions about license validation mechanism and other ambiguities.

observations.text: Exceptional rule decomposition — split cancellation into patient (BR-3) and admin (BR-4) rules, and note locking into threshold (BR-7) and immutability (BR-8), showing deep domain understanding
12 acceptance criteria with well-constructed Given/When/Then scenarios including boundary cases (AC-9 at 47h, AC-10 at 49h, AC-12 for cancelled slot reuse)

Invariant classifications are precise — BR-7 (48h threshold) marked false with sound justification that the specific window is policy, while BR-8 (immutability once locked) marked true as a state consistency invariant
Rich edge cases section identifies real ambiguities: license revocation post-verification, per-appointment vs per-patient record scoping, concurrent booking race conditions
Confidence level of 72 with well-articulated low-confidence areas and explicit assumptions about boundary conditions and license verification semantics
Escalations correctly identify the two most impactful ambiguities (record scoping and license revocation) with concrete options and recommendations

Domain analysis identifies a single bounded context ('Appointment & Consultation Management') rather than separately identifying 'appointment scheduling' and 'medical records access' as distinct bounded contexts as expected
